HTTP_PARAMETER_POLLUTION.LongDescription: Concatenating user-controlled input into a URL
HTTP_PARAMETER_POLLUTION.Details: Concatenating unvalidated user input into a URL can allow an attacker to override the value of a request parameter. Attacker may be able to override existing parameter values, inject a new parameter or exploit variables out of a direct reach. HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP) attacks consist of injecting encoded query string delimiters into other existing parameters. If a web application does not properly sanitize the user input, a malicious user may compromise the logic of the application to perform either client-side or server-side attacks. In the following example the programmer has not considered the possibility that an attacker could provide a parameter lang such as en&user_id=1, which would enable him to change the user_id at will.

SMTP_HEADER_INJECTION.LongDescription: Possible injection that can lead to Source spoofing, header override and email body injection.
SMTP_HEADER_INJECTION.Details: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) is a text based protocol used for email delivery. Like with HTTP, headers are separate by new line separator. If user input is place in a header line, the application should remove or replace new line characters (CR / LF). You should use a safe wrapper such as Apache Common Email and Simple Java Mail which filter special characters that can lead to header injection.

FORMAT_STRING_MANIPULATION.LongDescription: Format string argument allowing user controlled parameters
FORMAT_STRING_MANIPULATION.Details: Allowing user input to control format parameters could enable an attacker to cause exceptions to be thrown or leak information. Attackers may be able to modify the format string argument, such that an exception is thrown. If this exception is left uncaught, it may crash the application. Alternatively, if sensitive information is used within the unused arguments, attackers may change the format string to reveal this information. The example code below lets the user specify the decimal points to which it shows the balance. The user can in fact specify anything causing an exception to be thrown which could lead to application failure. Even more critical within this example, if an attacker can specify the user input "2f %3$s %4$.2", the format string would be "The customer: %s %s has the balance %4$.2f %3$s %4$.2". This would then lead to the sensitive accountNo to be included within the resulting string.

SPRING_FILE_DISCLOSURE.LongDescription: ModelAndView populated with user controlled parameters
SPRING_FILE_DISCLOSURE.Details: Constructing a server-side redirect path with user input could allow an attacker to download application binaries (including application classes or jar files) or view arbitrary files within protected directories. An attacker may be able to forge a request parameter to match sensitive file locations. For example, requesting "http://example.com/?returnURL=WEB-INF/applicationContext.xml" would display the application's applicationContext.xml file. The attacker would be able to locate and download the applicationContext.xml referenced in the other configuration files, and even class files or jar files, obtaining sensitive information and launching other types of attacks.

STRUTS_FILE_DISCLOSURE.LongDescription: ActionForward populated with user controlled parameters
STRUTS_FILE_DISCLOSURE.Details: Constructing a server-side redirect path with user input could allow an attacker to download application binaries (including application classes or jar files) or view arbitrary files within protected directories. An attacker may be able to forge a request parameter to match sensitive file locations. For example, requesting "http://example.com/?returnURL=WEB-INF/applicationContext.xml" would display the application's applicationContext.xml file. The attacker would be able to locate and download the applicationContext.xml referenced in the other configuration files, and even class files or jar files, obtaining sensitive information and launching other types of attacks.

REQUESTDISPATCHER_FILE_DISCLOSURE.LongDescription: RequestDispatcher populated with user controlled parameters
REQUESTDISPATCHER_FILE_DISCLOSURE.Details: Constructing a server-side redirect path with user input could allow an attacker to download application binaries (including application classes or jar files) or view arbitrary files within protected directories. An attacker may be able to forge a request parameter to match sensitive file locations. For example, requesting "http://example.com/?jspFile=../applicationContext.xml%3F" would display the application's applicationContext.xml file. The attacker would be able to locate and download the applicationContext.xml referenced in the other configuration files, and even class files or jar files, obtaining sensitive information and launching other types of attacks.

BEAN_PROPERTY_INJECTION.LongDescription: JavaBeans property name populated with user controlled parameters
BEAN_PROPERTY_INJECTION.Details: An attacker can set arbitrary bean properties that can compromise system integrity. Bean population functions allow to set a bean property or a nested property. An attacker can leverage this functionality to access special bean properties like class.classLoader that will allow him to override system properties and potentially execute arbitrary code.

AWS_QUERY_INJECTION.LongDescription: This SimpleDB client accepts data from an untrusted source.
AWS_QUERY_INJECTION.Details: Constructing SimpleDB queries containing user input can allow an attacker to view unauthorized records. The following example dynamically constructs and executes a SimpleDB SELECT query allowing the user to specify the productCategory. The attacker can modify the query, bypass the required authentication for customerID and view records matching any customer.

INSECURE_SMTP_SSL.LongDescription: This SMTP SSL conncection does not verify the server certificate
INSECURE_SMTP_SSL.Details: Server identity verification is disabled when making SSL connections. Some email libraries that enable SSL connections do not verify the server certificate by default. This is equivalent to trusting all certificates. When trying to connect to the server, this application would readily accept a certificate issued to "victim.com". The application would now potentially leak sensitive user information on a broken SSL connection to the victim server.

URL_REWRITING.LongDescription: Method rewriting session ID into the URL
URL_REWRITING.Details: The implementation of this method includes the logic to determine whether the session ID needs to be encoded in the URL. URL rewriting has significant security risks. Since session ID appears in the URL, it may be easily seen by third parties. Session ID in the URL can be disclosed in many ways, for example: Log files, The browser history, By copy-and-pasting it into an e-mail or posting, The HTTP Referrer.

COOKIE_PERSISTENT.LongDescription: Cookie Set To Expire In 1 Year Or More
COOKIE_PERSISTENT.Details: Storing sensitive data in a persistent cookie for an extended period can lead to a breach of confidentiality or account compromise.

PERMISSIVE_CORS.LongDescription: The program defines an overly permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) policy
PERMISSIVE_CORS.Details: Prior to HTML5, Web browsers enforced the Same Origin Policy which ensures that in order for JavaScript to access the contents of a Web page, both the JavaScript and the Web page must originate from the same domain. Without the Same Origin Policy, a malicious website could serve up JavaScript that loads sensitive information from other websites using a client's credentials, cull through it, and communicate it back to the attacker. HTML5 makes it possible for JavaScript to access data across domains if a new HTTP header called Access-Control-Allow-Origin is defined. With this header, a Web server defines which other domains are allowed to access its domain using cross-origin requests. However, caution should be taken when defining the header because an overly permissive CORS policy will allow a malicious application to communicate with the victim application in an inappropriate way, leading to spoofing, data theft, relay and other attacks.

PREDICTABLE_RANDOM.Details: The use of a predictable random value can lead to vulnerabilities when used in certain security critical contexts.

PREDICTABLE_RANDOM_SCALA.LongDescription: This Scala random generator ({3}) is predictable
PREDICTABLE_RANDOM_SCALA.Details: The use of a predictable random value can lead to vulnerabilities when used in certain security critical contexts.

SERVLET_PARAMETER.LongDescription: The method {3} returns a String value that is controlled by the client
SERVLET_PARAMETER.Details: The Servlet can read GET and POST parameters from various methods. The value obtained should be considered unsafe. You may need to validate or sanitize those values before passing them to sensitive APIs such as:

SERVLET_CONTENT_TYPE.LongDescription: The HTTP header Content-Type can be controlled by the client
SERVLET_CONTENT_TYPE.Details: The HTTP header Content-Type can be controlled by the client. As such, its value should not be used in any security critical decisions.

SERVLET_SERVER_NAME.LongDescription: The hostname received can often be controlled by the client
SERVLET_SERVER_NAME.Details: The hostname header can be controlled by the client. As such, its value should not be used in any security critical decisions. Both ServletRequest.getServerName() and HttpServletRequest.getHeader("Host") have the same behavior which is to extract the Host header.

SERVLET_SESSION_ID.LongDescription: Direct access to Session ID should be avoided
SERVLET_SESSION_ID.Details: The method HttpServletRequest.getRequestedSessionId() typically returns the value of the cookie JSESSIONID. This value is normally only accessed by the session management logic and not normal developer code.

SERVLET_QUERY_STRING.LongDescription: The query string can be any value
SERVLET_QUERY_STRING.Details: The query string is the concatenation of the GET parameter names and values. Parameters other than those intended can be passed in.

SERVLET_HEADER.LongDescription: Request header can easily be altered by the client
SERVLET_HEADER.Details: Request headers can easily be altered by the requesting user. In general, no assumption should be made that the request came from a regular browser without modification by an attacker. As such, it is recommended that you not trust this value in any security decisions you make with respect to a request.

SERVLET_HEADER_REFERER.LongDescription: The header "Referer" can easily be spoofed by the client
SERVLET_HEADER_REFERER.Details: Behavior: Any value can be assigned to this header if the request is coming from a malicious user. The "Referer" will not be present if the request was initiated from another origin that is secure (HTTPS).

SERVLET_HEADER_USER_AGENT.LongDescription: The header "User-Agent" can easily be spoofed by the client
SERVLET_HEADER_USER_AGENT.Details: The header "User-Agent" can easily be spoofed by the client. Adopting different behaviors based on the User-Agent (for crawler UA) is not recommended.

COOKIE_USAGE.ShortDescription: Potentially sensitive data in a cookie
COOKIE_USAGE.LongDescription: Sensitive data may be stored by the application in a cookie
COOKIE_USAGE.Details: The information stored in a custom cookie should not be sensitive or related to the session. In most cases, sensitive data should only be stored in session and referenced by the user's session cookie. See HttpSession (HttpServletRequest.getSession())

INSECURE_COOKIE.LongDescription: Cookie without the secure flag could be sent in clear text if an HTTP URL is visited
INSECURE_COOKIE.Details: A new cookie is created without the Secure flag set. The Secure flag is a directive to the browser to make sure that the cookie is not sent for insecure communication (http://).

HTTPONLY_COOKIE.LongDescription: Cookie without the HttpOnly flag could be read by a malicious script in the browser
HTTPONLY_COOKIE.Details: A new cookie is created without the HttpOnly flag set. The HttpOnly flag is a directive to the browser to make sure that the cookie can not be red by malicious script. When a user is the target of a "Cross-Site Scripting", the attacker would benefit greatly from getting the session id for example.

PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN.LongDescription: This API ({3}) reads a file whose location might be specified by user input
PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN.Details: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location could be read.

PATH_TRAVERSAL_OUT.LongDescription: This API ({3}) writes to a file whose location might be specified by user input
PATH_TRAVERSAL_OUT.Details: A file is opened to write to its contents. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files at an arbitrary filesystem location could be modified.

SCALA_PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN.ShortDescription: Potential Path Traversal using Scala API (file read)
SCALA_PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN.LongDescription: This Scala API ({3}) reads a file whose location might be specified by user input
SCALA_PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN.Details: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location could be read.

COMMAND_INJECTION.LongDescription: This usage of {3} can be vulnerable to Command Injection
COMMAND_INJECTION.Details: The highlighted API is used to execute a system command. If unfiltered input is passed to this API, it can lead to arbitrary command execution.

SCALA_COMMAND_INJECTION.LongDescription: The command execution could be vulnerable to injection
SCALA_COMMAND_INJECTION.Details: The highlighted API is used to execute a system command. If unfiltered input is passed to this API, it can lead to arbitrary command execution.

WEAK_FILENAMEUTILS.Details: Some FilenameUtils' methods don't filter NULL bytes (0x00).

WEAK_TRUST_MANAGER.LongDescription: TrustManager that accept any certificates makes communication vulnerable to a MITM attack
WEAK_TRUST_MANAGER.Details: Empty TrustManager implementations are often used to connect easily to a host that is not signed by a root certificate authority. As a consequence, this is vulnerable to Man-in-the-middle attacks since the client will trust any certificate.

WEAK_HOSTNAME_VERIFIER.ShortDescription: HostnameVerifier that accept any signed certificates
WEAK_HOSTNAME_VERIFIER.LongDescription: HostnameVerifier that accept any signed certificates makes communication vulnerable to a MITM attack
WEAK_HOSTNAME_VERIFIER.Details: A HostnameVerifier that accept any host are often use because of certificate reuse on many hosts. As a consequence, this is vulnerable to Man-in-the-middle attacks since the client will trust any certificate.

JAXWS_ENDPOINT.LongDescription: {0}.{1} is a SOAP Web Service endpoint
JAXWS_ENDPOINT.Details: This method is part of a SOAP Web Service (JSR224).

JAXRS_ENDPOINT.LongDescription: {0}.{1} is a REST Web Service endpoint
JAXRS_ENDPOINT.Details: This method is part of a REST Web Service (JSR311).

TAPESTRY_ENDPOINT.Details: A Tapestry endpoint was discovered at application startup. Tapestry apps are structured with a backing Java class and a corresponding Tapestry Markup Language page (a .tml file) for each page. When a request is received, the GET/POST parameters are mapped to specific inputs in the backing Java class. The mapping is either done with field name:

WICKET_ENDPOINT.Details: This class represents a Wicket WebPage. Input is automatically read from a PageParameters instance passed to the constructor. The current page is mapped to the view /package/WebPageName.html.

WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_MD5.ShortDescription: MD2, MD4 and MD5 are weak hash functions
WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_MD5.LongDescription: This API {3} (MDX) is not a recommended cryptographic hash function
WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_MD5.Details: The algorithms MD2, MD4 and MD5 are not a recommended MessageDigest. PBKDF2 should be used to hash password for example.

WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_SHA1.ShortDescription: SHA-1 is a weak hash function
WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_SHA1.LongDescription: This API {3} (SHA-1) is not a recommended cryptographic hash function
WEAK_MESSAGE_DIGEST_SHA1.Details: The algorithms SHA-1 is not a recommended algorithm for hash password, for signature verification and other uses. PBKDF2 should be used to hash password for example.

DEFAULT_HTTP_CLIENT.ShortDescription: DefaultHttpClient with default constructor is not compatible with TLS 1.2
DEFAULT_HTTP_CLIENT.LongDescription: DefaultHttpClient with default constructor is not compatible with TLS 1.2
DEFAULT_HTTP_CLIENT.Details: Vulnerable Code: HttpClient client = new DefaultHttpClient();

SSL_CONTEXT.LongDescription: SSLContext needs to be compatible with TLS 1.2

CUSTOM_MESSAGE_DIGEST.LongDescription: {0} is a custom cryptographic hash function implementation
CUSTOM_MESSAGE_DIGEST.Details: Implementing a custom MessageDigest is error-prone.

FILE_UPLOAD_FILENAME.LongDescription: The filename read can be tampered with by the client
FILE_UPLOAD_FILENAME.Details: The filename provided by the FileUpload API can be tampered with by the client to reference unauthorized files.

REDOS.LongDescription: The regular expression "{0}" is vulnerable to a denial of service attack (ReDOS)
REDOS.Details: Regular expressions (Regex) are frequently subject to Denial of Service (DOS) attacks (called ReDOS). This is due to the fact that regex engines may take a large amount of time when analyzing certain strings, depending on how the regex is defined. For example, for the regex: ^(a+)+$, the input "aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaX" will cause the regex engine to analyze 65536 different paths.[1] Example taken from OWASP references

XXE_SAXPARSER.LongDescription: The use of {3} (SAXParser) is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_SAXPARSER.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_XMLREADER.LongDescription: The use of {3} (XMLReader) is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_XMLREADER.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_DOCUMENT.LongDescription: The use of {3} (DocumentBuilder) is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_DOCUMENT.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_DTD_TRANSFORM_FACTORY.LongDescription: The use of {3} (TransformerFactory) is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_DTD_TRANSFORM_FACTORY.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_SCHEMA_FACTORY.ShortDescription: XML schema processing vulnerable to XXE
XXE_SCHEMA_FACTORY.LongDescription: XML schema processing is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_SCHEMA_FACTORY.Details: XML External Entity attacks can occur when an XML SchemaFactory supports access to external entity references or external schema locations while parsing XML Schema Documents.

XXE_XSLT_TRANSFORM_FACTORY.LongDescription: The use of {3} is vulnerable to XSLT External Entity attacks
XXE_XSLT_TRANSFORM_FACTORY.Details: XSLT External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XSLT parser supports external entities while processing XSLT received from an untrusted source.

XXE_XMLSTREAMREADER.LongDescription: The XML parsing is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_XMLSTREAMREADER.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_XPATH.LongDescription: The use of {3} (XPath API) is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_XPATH.Details: XML External Entity (XXE) attacks can occur when an XML parser supports XML entities while processing XML received from an untrusted source.

XXE_VALIDATOR.LongDescription: XML validation is vulnerable to XML External Entity attacks
XXE_VALIDATOR.Details: XML External Entity attacks can occur when an XML Validator supports access to external entity references or external schema locations while validating malicious sources.

XPATH_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to XPath Injection
XPATH_INJECTION.Details: XPath injection risks are similar to SQL injection. If the XPath query contains untrusted user input, the complete data source could be exposed. This could allow an attacker to access unauthorized data or maliciously modify the target XML.

STRUTS1_ENDPOINT.Details: This class is a Struts 1 Action.

STRUTS2_ENDPOINT.Details: In Struts 2, the endpoints are Plain Old Java Objects (POJO) which means no Interface/Class needs to be implemented/extended.

SPRING_ENDPOINT.Details: This class is a Spring Controller. All methods annotated with RequestMapping (as well as its shortcut annotations GetMapping, PostMapping, PutMapping, DeleteMapping, and PatchMapping) are reachable remotely. This class should be analyzed to make sure that remotely exposed methods are safe to expose to potential attackers.

SPRING_CSRF_PROTECTION_DISABLED.LongDescription: Disabling Spring Security's CSRF protection is unsafe for standard web applications
SPRING_CSRF_PROTECTION_DISABLED.Details: Disabling Spring Security's CSRF protection is unsafe for standard web applications.

SPRING_CSRF_UNRESTRICTED_REQUEST_MAPPING.LongDescription: Unrestricted Spring's RequestMapping makes the method vulnerable to CSRF attacks
SPRING_CSRF_UNRESTRICTED_REQUEST_MAPPING.Details: Methods annotated with RequestMapping are by default mapped to all the HTTP request methods. However, Spring Security's CSRF protection is not enabled by default for the HTTP request methods GET, HEAD, TRACE, and OPTIONS (as this could cause the tokens to be leaked). Therefore, state-changing methods annotated with RequestMapping and not narrowing the mapping to the HTTP request methods POST, PUT, DELETE, or PATCH are vulnerable to CSRF attacks.

SAML_IGNORE_COMMENTS.ShortDescription: Ignoring XML comments in SAML may lead to authentication bypass
SAML_IGNORE_COMMENTS.LongDescription: Ignoring XML comments in Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) may lead to authentication bypass
SAML_IGNORE_COMMENTS.Details: Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) is a single sign-on protocol that that used XML. The SAMLResponse message include statements that describe the authenticated user. If a user manage to place XML comments (<!-- -->), it may caused issue in the way the parser extract literal value.

CUSTOM_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to injection
CUSTOM_INJECTION.Details: The method identified is susceptible to injection. The input should be validated and properly escaped.

SQL_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection
SQL_INJECTION.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection. Alternatively to prepare statements, each parameter can be escaped manually.

SQL_INJECTION_VERTX.ShortDescription: Potential SQL Injection with Vert.x Sql Client
SQL_INJECTION_VERTX.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Vert.x Sql Client)
SQL_INJECTION_VERTX.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection. Vert.x Sql Client API provide a DSL to build query with Java code.

SQL_INJECTION_TURBINE.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Turbine)
SQL_INJECTION_TURBINE.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection. Turbine API provide a DSL to build query with Java code.

SQL_INJECTION_HIBERNATE.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL/HQL injection (with Hibernate)
SQL_INJECTION_HIBERNATE.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection. Alternatively to prepare statements, Hibernate Criteria can be used.

SQL_INJECTION_JDO.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL/JDOQL injection (with JDO)
SQL_INJECTION_JDO.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SQL_INJECTION_JPA.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL/JPQL injection (with JPA)
SQL_INJECTION_JPA.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SQL_INJECTION_SPRING_JDBC.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Spring JDBC)
SQL_INJECTION_SPRING_JDBC.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SQL_INJECTION_JDBC.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with JDBC)
SQL_INJECTION_JDBC.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SQL_INJECTION_ANDROID.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Android SQL)
SQL_INJECTION_ANDROID.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SCALA_SQL_INJECTION_SLICK.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Slick)
SCALA_SQL_INJECTION_SLICK.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

SCALA_SQL_INJECTION_ANORM.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to SQL injection (with Anorm)
SCALA_SQL_INJECTION_ANORM.Details: The input values included in SQL queries need to be passed in safely. Bind variables in prepared statements can be used to easily mitigate the risk of SQL injection.

LDAP_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to LDAP injection
LDAP_INJECTION.Details: Just like SQL, all inputs passed to an LDAP query need to be passed in safely. Unfortunately, LDAP doesn't have prepared statement interfaces like SQL. Therefore, the primary defense against LDAP injection is strong input validation of any untrusted data before including it in an LDAP query.

LDAP_ANONYMOUS.Details: Without proper access control, executing an LDAP statement that contains a user-controlled value can allow an attacker to abuse poorly configured LDAP context. All LDAP queries executed against the context will be performed without authentication and access control. An attacker may be able to manipulate one of these queries in an unexpected way to gain access to records that would otherwise be protected by the directory's access control mechanism.

LDAP_ENTRY_POISONING.Details: JNDI API support the binding of serialize object in LDAP directories. If certain attributes are presented, the deserialization of object will be made in the application querying the directory (See Black Hat USA 2016 white paper for details). Object deserialization should be consider a risky operation that can lead to remote code execution.

BAD_HEXA_CONVERSION.LongDescription: Leading zeros are omitted in the concatenation increasing collision potential
BAD_HEXA_CONVERSION.Details: When converting a byte array containing a hash signature to a human readable string, a conversion mistake can be made if the array is read byte by byte. The following sample illustrates the use of the method Integer.toHexString() which will trim any leading zeroes from each byte of the computed hash value. MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256"); byte[] resultBytes = md.digest(password.getBytes("UTF-8")); StringBuilder stringBuilder = new StringBuilder(); for(byte b :resultBytes) { stringBuilder.append( Integer.toHexString( b & 0xFF ) ); } return stringBuilder.toString();

HAZELCAST_SYMMETRIC_ENCRYPTION.LongDescription: The network communication for Hazelcast are configured to use a symmetric cipher
HAZELCAST_SYMMETRIC_ENCRYPTION.Details: The network communications for Hazelcast is configured to use a symmetric cipher (probably DES or Blowfish).

NULL_CIPHER.LongDescription: The use of a NullCipher is typically not desirable
NULL_CIPHER.Details: The NullCipher is rarely used intentionally in production applications. It implements the Cipher interface by returning ciphertext identical to the supplied plaintext. In a few contexts, such as testing, a NullCipher may be appropriate.

UNENCRYPTED_SOCKET.Details: The communication channel used is not encrypted. The traffic could be read by an attacker intercepting the network traffic.

UNENCRYPTED_SERVER_SOCKET.Details: The communication channel used is not encrypted. The traffic could be read by an attacker intercepting the network traffic.

DES_USAGE.LongDescription: DES should be replaced with AES
DES_USAGE.Details: DES is considered strong ciphers for modern applications. Currently, NIST recommends the usage of AES block ciphers instead of DES.

TDES_USAGE.LongDescription: DESede should be replaced with AES
TDES_USAGE.Details: Triple DES (also known as 3DES or DESede) is considered strong ciphers for modern applications. Currently, NIST recommends the usage of AES block ciphers instead of 3DES.

RSA_NO_PADDING.ShortDescription: RSA with no padding is insecure
RSA_NO_PADDING.LongDescription: Use of RSA cipher without proper padding
RSA_NO_PADDING.Details: The software uses the RSA algorithm but does not incorporate Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding (OAEP), which might weaken the encryption.

HARD_CODE_PASSWORD.Details: Passwords should not be kept in the source code. The source code can be widely shared in an enterprise environment, and is certainly shared in open source. To be managed safely, passwords and secret keys should be stored in separate configuration files or keystores. (Hard coded keys are reported separately by Hard Coded Key pattern)

UNSAFE_HASH_EQUALS.LongDescription: Unsafe comparison of hash that are susceptible to timing attack
UNSAFE_HASH_EQUALS.Details: An attacker might be able to detect the value of the secret hash due to the exposure of comparison timing. When the functions Arrays.equals() or String.equals() are called, they will exit earlier if fewer bytes are matched.

HARD_CODE_KEY.Details: Cryptographic keys should not be kept in the source code. The source code can be widely shared in an enterprise environment, and is certainly shared in open source. To be managed safely, passwords and secret keys should be stored in separate configuration files or keystores. (Hard coded passwords are reported separately by the Hard coded password pattern)

STRUTS_FORM_VALIDATION.LongDescription: Struts Form with no input validation
STRUTS_FORM_VALIDATION.Details: Form inputs should have minimal input validation. Preventive validation helps provide defense in depth against a variety of risks.

XSS_REQUEST_WRAPPER.ShortDescription: XSSRequestWrapper is a weak XSS protection
XSS_REQUEST_WRAPPER.LongDescription: XSSRequestWrapper is a weak XSS protection mechanism
XSS_REQUEST_WRAPPER.Details: An implementation of HttpServletRequestWrapper called XSSRequestWrapper was published through various blog sites. [1] [2]

RSA_KEY_SIZE.LongDescription: RSA usage with a short key
RSA_KEY_SIZE.Details: The NIST recommends the use of 2048 bits and higher keys for the RSA algorithm.

BLOWFISH_KEY_SIZE.LongDescription: Blowfish usage with a short encryption key
BLOWFISH_KEY_SIZE.Details: The Blowfish cipher supports key sizes from 32 bits to 448 bits. A small key size makes the ciphertext vulnerable to brute force attacks. At least 128 bits of entropy should be used when generating the key if the use of Blowfish is required.

UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.LongDescription: The following redirection could be used by an attacker to redirect users to a phishing website.
UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.Details: Unvalidated redirects occur when an application redirects a user to a destination URL specified by a user supplied parameter that is not validated. Such vulnerabilities can be used to facilitate phishing attacks.

PLAY_UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.LongDescription: The following redirection could be used by an attacker to redirect users to a phishing website. (in the context of the Play Framework)
PLAY_UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.Details: Unvalidated redirects occur when an application redirects a user to a destination URL specified by a user supplied parameter that is not validated. Such vulnerabilities can be used to facilitate phishing attacks.

SPRING_UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.LongDescription: The following redirection could be used by an attacker to redirect users to a phishing website. (in the context of a Spring application)
SPRING_UNVALIDATED_REDIRECT.Details: Unvalidated redirects occur when an application redirects a user to a destination URL specified by a user supplied parameter that is not validated. Such vulnerabilities can be used to facilitate phishing attacks.

ENTITY_LEAK.LongDescription: Unexpected property could be leaked because a persistence class is directly exposed to the client
ENTITY_LEAK.Details: Persistent objects should never be returned by APIs. They might lead to leaking business logic over the UI, unauthorized tampering of persistent objects in database.

ENTITY_MASS_ASSIGNMENT.LongDescription: Persistent objects as MVC parameter could allow attacker to set fields that the developer never intended to be set via request parameters.
ENTITY_MASS_ASSIGNMENT.Details: Software frameworks sometime allow developers to automatically bind HTTP request parameters into program code variables or objects to make using that framework easier on developers. This can sometimes cause harm.

XSS_JSP_PRINT.Details: A potential XSS was found. It could be used to execute unwanted JavaScript in a client's browser. (See references)

JSP_INCLUDE.LongDescription: Dynamic JSP inclusion could lead to arbitrary code execution
JSP_INCLUDE.Details: The inclusion of JSP file allow the entry of dynamic value. It may allow an attacker to control the JSP page included. If this is the case, an attacker will try to include a file on disk that he controls. By including arbitrary files, the attacker gets the ability to execute any code.

JSP_SPRING_EVAL.LongDescription: Dynamic variable in Spring expression could lead to arbitrary code execution
JSP_SPRING_EVAL.Details: A Spring expression is built with a dynamic value. The source of the value(s) should be verified to avoid that unfiltered values fall into this risky code evaluation.

JSP_JSTL_OUT.ShortDescription: Escaping of special XML characters is disabled
JSP_JSTL_OUT.LongDescription: Disabling the escaping of special XML characters can lead to XSS vulnerabilities
JSP_JSTL_OUT.Details: A potential XSS was found. It could be used to execute unwanted JavaScript in a client's browser. (See references)

JSP_XSLT.ShortDescription: A malicious XSLT could be provided to the JSP tag
JSP_XSLT.LongDescription: A malicious XSLT could be provided to the JSP tag to trigger remote code execution
JSP_XSLT.Details: "XSLT (Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations) is a language for transforming XML documents into other XML documents".[1] It is possible to attach malicious behavior to those style sheets. Therefore, if an attacker can control the content or the source of the style sheet, he might be able to trigger remote code execution.[2]

MALICIOUS_XSLT.ShortDescription: A malicious XSLT could be provided
MALICIOUS_XSLT.LongDescription: A malicious XSLT could be provided to trigger remote code execution
MALICIOUS_XSLT.Details: "XSLT (Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations) is a language for transforming XML documents into other XML documents".[1] It is possible to attach malicious behavior to those style sheets. Therefore, if an attacker can control the content or the source of the style sheet, he might be able to trigger remote code execution.[2]

XSS_SERVLET.LongDescription: This use of {3} could be vulnerable to XSS in the Servlet
XSS_SERVLET.Details: A potential XSS was found. It could be used to execute unwanted JavaScript in a client's browser. (See references)

XML_DECODER.LongDescription: It is not safe to use an XMLDecoder to parse user supplied data
XML_DECODER.Details: XMLDecoder should not be used to parse untrusted data. Deserializing user input can lead to arbitrary code execution. This is possible because XMLDecoder supports arbitrary method invocation. This capability is intended to call setter methods, but in practice, any method can be called.

STATIC_IV.LongDescription: The initialization vector (IV) is not properly generated
STATIC_IV.Details: Initialization vector must be regenerated for each message to be encrypted.

ECB_MODE.LongDescription: The cipher uses ECB mode, which provides poor confidentiality for encrypted data
ECB_MODE.Details: An authentication cipher mode which provides better confidentiality of the encrypted data should be used instead of Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode, which does not provide good confidentiality. Specifically, ECB mode produces the same output for the same input each time. So, for example, if a user is sending a password, the encrypted value is the same each time. This allows an attacker to intercept and replay the data.

PADDING_ORACLE.ShortDescription: Cipher is susceptible to Padding Oracle
PADDING_ORACLE.LongDescription: The cipher is susceptible to padding oracle attacks
PADDING_ORACLE.Details: This specific mode of CBC with PKCS5Padding is susceptible to padding oracle attacks. An adversary could potentially decrypt the message if the system exposed the difference between plaintext with invalid padding or valid padding. The distinction between valid and invalid padding is usually revealed through distinct error messages being returned for each condition.

CIPHER_INTEGRITY.LongDescription: The cipher does not provide data integrity
CIPHER_INTEGRITY.Details: The ciphertext produced is susceptible to alteration by an adversary. This mean that the cipher provides no way to detect that the data has been tampered with. If the ciphertext can be controlled by an attacker, it could be altered without detection.

ESAPI_ENCRYPTOR.LongDescription: The ESAPI encryptor API is used to encrypt data
ESAPI_ENCRYPTOR.Details: The ESAPI has a small history of vulnerabilities within the cryptography component. Here is a quick validation list to make sure the Authenticated Encryption is working as expected.

SCRIPT_ENGINE_INJECTION.ShortDescription: Potential code injection when using Script Engine
SCRIPT_ENGINE_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} can be vulnerable to code injection
SCRIPT_ENGINE_INJECTION.Details: Dynamic code is being evaluated. A careful analysis of the code construction should be made. Malicious code execution could lead to data leakage or operating system compromised.

SPEL_INJECTION.ShortDescription: Potential code injection when using Spring Expression
SPEL_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} could be vulnerable to code injection (Spring Expression)
SPEL_INJECTION.Details: A Spring expression is built with a dynamic value. The source of the value(s) should be verified to avoid that unfiltered values fall into this risky code evaluation.

EL_INJECTION.ShortDescription: Potential code injection when using Expression Language (EL)
EL_INJECTION.LongDescription: This use of {3} could be vulnerable to code injection (Expression Language)
EL_INJECTION.Details: An expression is built with a dynamic value. The source of the value(s) should be verified to avoid that unfiltered values fall into this risky code evaluation.

SEAM_LOG_INJECTION.ShortDescription: Potential code injection in Seam logging call
SEAM_LOG_INJECTION.LongDescription: Potential code injection in Seam logging call
SEAM_LOG_INJECTION.Details: Seam Logging API support an expression language to introduce bean property to log messages. The expression language can also be the source to unwanted code execution.

OGNL_INJECTION.ShortDescription: Potential code injection when using OGNL expression
OGNL_INJECTION.LongDescription: Potential code injection when using OGNL expression
OGNL_INJECTION.Details: A expression is built with a dynamic value. The source of the value(s) should be verified to avoid that unfiltered values fall into this risky code evaluation.

GROOVY_SHELL.ShortDescription: Potential code injection when using GroovyShell
GROOVY_SHELL.LongDescription: Potential code injection when using GroovyShell
GROOVY_SHELL.Details: A expression is built with a dynamic value. The source of the value(s) should be verified to avoid that unfiltered values fall into this risky code evaluation.

HTTP_RESPONSE_SPLITTING.LongDescription: This use of {3} might be used to include CRLF characters into HTTP headers
HTTP_RESPONSE_SPLITTING.Details: When an HTTP request contains unexpected CR and LF characters, the server may respond with an output stream that is interpreted as two different HTTP responses (instead of one). An attacker can control the second response and mount attacks such as cross-site scripting and cache poisoning attacks. According to OWASP, the issue has been fixed in virtually all modern Java EE application servers, but it is still better to validate the input. If you are concerned about this risk, you should test on the platform of concern to see if the underlying platform allows for CR or LF characters to be injected into headers. This weakness is reported with low priority because it requires the web container to be vulnerable.

CRLF_INJECTION_LOGS.LongDescription: This use of {3} might be used to include CRLF characters into log messages
CRLF_INJECTION_LOGS.Details: When data from an untrusted source is put into a logger and not neutralized correctly, an attacker could forge log entries or include malicious content. Inserted false entries could be used to skew statistics, distract the administrator or even to implicate another party in the commission of a malicious act. If the log file is processed automatically, the attacker can render the file unusable by corrupting the format of the file or injecting unexpected characters. An attacker may also inject code or other commands into the log file and take advantage of a vulnerability in the log processing utility (e.g. command injection or XSS).

EXTERNAL_CONFIG_CONTROL.LongDescription: This use of {3} might be used to control configuration externally
EXTERNAL_CONFIG_CONTROL.Details: Allowing external control of system settings can disrupt service or cause an application to behave in unexpected, and potentially malicious ways. An attacker could cause an error by providing a nonexistent catalog name or connect to an unauthorized portion of the database.

ANDROID_EXTERNAL_FILE_ACCESS.LongDescription: Files could be saved to external storage
ANDROID_EXTERNAL_FILE_ACCESS.Details: The application write data to external storage (potentially SD card). There are multiple security implication to this action. First, file store on SD card will be accessible to the application having the READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permission. Also, if the data persisted contains confidential information about the user, encryption would be needed.

ANDROID_BROADCAST.LongDescription: Broadcast intents could be received by a malicious application
ANDROID_BROADCAST.Details: Broadcast intents can be listened by any application with the appropriate permission. It is suggested to avoid transmitting sensitive information when possible.

ANDROID_WORLD_WRITABLE.LongDescription: The content that is written can be viewed by any application
ANDROID_WORLD_WRITABLE.Details: The file written in this context is using the creation mode MODE_WORLD_READABLE. It might not be the expected behavior to expose the content being written.

ANDROID_GEOLOCATION.Details: It is suggested to ask the user for a confirmation about obtaining its geolocation.

ANDROID_WEB_VIEW_JAVASCRIPT.Details: Enabling JavaScript for the WebView means that it is now susceptible to XSS. The page render should be inspected for potential reflected XSS, stored XSS and DOM XSS. WebView myWebView = (WebView) findViewById(R.id.webView); WebSettings webSettings = myWebView.getSettings(); webSettings.setJavaScriptEnabled(true);

ANDROID_WEB_VIEW_JAVASCRIPT_INTERFACE.Details: The use of JavaScript Interface could expose the WebView to risky API. If an XSS is triggered in the WebView, the class could be called by the malicious JavaScript code.

OBJECT_DESERIALIZATION.Details: Object deserialization of untrusted data can lead to remote code execution, if there is a class in classpath that allows the trigger of malicious operation.

JACKSON_UNSAFE_DESERIALIZATION.LongDescription: Unsafe Jackson deserialization configuration used in {1} {2} {3}
JACKSON_UNSAFE_DESERIALIZATION.Details: When the Jackson databind library is used incorrectly the deserialization of untrusted data can lead to remote code execution, if there is a class in classpath that allows the trigger of malicious operation. Solutions: Explicitly define what types and subtypes you want to be available when using polymorphism through JsonTypeInfo.Id.NAME. Also, never call ObjectMapper.enableDefaultTyping (and then readValue a type that holds an Object or Serializable or Comparable or a known deserialization type).

DESERIALIZATION_GADGET.ShortDescription: This class could be used as deserialization gadget
DESERIALIZATION_GADGET.LongDescription: This class could make application using serialization vulnerable
DESERIALIZATION_GADGET.Details: Deserialization gadget are class that could be used by an attacker to take advantage of a remote API using Native Serialization. This class is either adding custom behavior to deserialization with the readObject method (Serializable) or can be called from a serialized object (InvocationHandler).

TRUST_BOUNDARY_VIOLATION.LongDescription: The application mixes trusted and untrusted data in session attributes.
TRUST_BOUNDARY_VIOLATION.Details: "A trust boundary can be thought of as line drawn through a program. On one side of the line, data is untrusted. On the other side of the line, data is assumed to be trustworthy. The purpose of validation logic is to allow data to safely cross the trust boundary - to move from untrusted to trusted. A trust boundary violation occurs when a program blurs the line between what is trusted and what is untrusted. By combining trusted and untrusted data in the same data structure, it becomes easier for programmers to mistakenly trust unvalidated data." [1]

SCALA_SENSITIVE_DATA_EXPOSURE.ShortDescription: Potential information leakage in Scala Play
SCALA_SENSITIVE_DATA_EXPOSURE.LongDescription: Configuration or system information leakage found in Scala Play
SCALA_SENSITIVE_DATA_EXPOSURE.Details: Applications can unintentionally leak information about their configuration, internal workings, or violate privacy through a variety of application problems. [1] Pages that provide different responses based on the validity of the data can lead to Information Leakage; specifically when data deemed confidential is being revealed as a result of the web application's design. [2]

SCALA_PLAY_SSRF.LongDescription: This web server request could be used by an attacker to expose internal services.
SCALA_PLAY_SSRF.Details: Server-Side Request Forgery occur when a web server executes a request to a user supplied destination parameter that is not validated. Such vulnerabilities could allow an attacker to access internal services or to launch attacks from your web server.

SCALA_XSS_TWIRL.ShortDescription: Potential XSS in Scala Twirl template engine
SCALA_XSS_TWIRL.LongDescription: This use of {3} could be vulnerable to XSS in the Twirl template
SCALA_XSS_TWIRL.Details: A potential XSS was found. It could be used to execute unwanted JavaScript in a client's browser. (See references)

SCALA_XSS_MVC_API.ShortDescription: Potential XSS in Scala MVC API engine
SCALA_XSS_MVC_API.LongDescription: A MVC result with this content-type could be vulnerable to XSS
SCALA_XSS_MVC_API.Details: A potential XSS was found. It could be used to execute unwanted JavaScript in a client's browser. (See references)

TEMPLATE_INJECTION_VELOCITY.LongDescription: Potential template injection with Velocity template
TEMPLATE_INJECTION_VELOCITY.Details: Velocity template engine is powerful. It is possible to add logic including condition statements, loops and external calls. It is not designed to be a sandbox to templating operations. A malicious user in control of a template can run malicious code on the server-side. Velocity templates should be seen as scripts.

TEMPLATE_INJECTION_FREEMARKER.LongDescription: Potential template injection with Freemarker template
TEMPLATE_INJECTION_FREEMARKER.Details: Freemarker template engine is powerful. It is possible to add logic including condition statements, loops and external calls. It is not design to be sandbox to templating operations. A malicious user in control of a template can run malicious code on the server-side. Freemarker templates should be seen as scripts.

TEMPLATE_INJECTION_PEBBLE.LongDescription: Potential template injection with Pebble template
TEMPLATE_INJECTION_PEBBLE.Details: Pebble template engine is powerful. It is possible to add logic including condition statements, loops and external calls. It is not design to be sandbox to templating operations. A malicious user in control of a template can run malicious code on the server-side. Pebble templates should be seen as scripts.

URLCONNECTION_SSRF_FD.ShortDescription: URLConnection Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) and File Disclosure
URLCONNECTION_SSRF_FD.LongDescription: This web server request could be used by an attacker to expose internal services and filesystem.
URLCONNECTION_SSRF_FD.Details: Server-Side Request Forgery occur when a web server executes a request to a user supplied destination parameter that is not validated. Such vulnerabilities could allow an attacker to access internal services or to launch attacks from your web server.

INFORMATION_EXPOSURE_THROUGH_AN_ERROR_ME.ShortDescription: Information Exposure Through An Error Message
INFORMATION_EXPOSURE_THROUGH_AN_ERROR_ME.LongDescription: Possible information exposure through an error message
INFORMATION_EXPOSURE_THROUGH_AN_ERROR_ME.Details: The sensitive information may be valuable information on its own (such as a password), or it may be useful for launching other, more deadly attacks. If an attack fails, an attacker may use error information provided by the server to launch another more focused attack. For example, an attempt to exploit a path traversal weakness (CWE-22) might yield the full pathname of the installed application. In turn, this could be used to select the proper number of ".." sequences to navigate to the targeted file. An attack using SQL injection (CWE-89) might not initially succeed, but an error message could reveal the malformed query, which would expose query logic and possibly even passwords or other sensitive information used within the query.

RPC_ENABLED_EXTENSIONS.ShortDescription: Enabling extensions in Apache XML RPC server or client.
RPC_ENABLED_EXTENSIONS.LongDescription: It is not safe to enable extensions in Apache XML RPC server or client
RPC_ENABLED_EXTENSIONS.Details: Enabling extensions in Apache XML RPC server or client can lead to deserialization vulnerability which would allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. It's recommended not to use setEnabledForExtensions method of org.apache.xmlrpc.client.XmlRpcClientConfigImpl or org.apache.xmlrpc.XmlRpcConfigImpl. By default, extensions are disabled both on the client and the server.

WICKET_XSS1.ShortDescription: Disabling HTML escaping put the application at risk for XSS
WICKET_XSS1.LongDescription: Disabling HTML escaping put the application at risk for XSS
WICKET_XSS1.Details: Disabling HTML escaping put the application at risk for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

OVERLY_PERMISSIVE_FILE_PERMISSION.LongDescription: Overly permissive file permission can lead to privilege escalation or information leakage.
OVERLY_PERMISSIVE_FILE_PERMISSION.Details: It is generally a bad practices to set overly permissive file permission such as read+write+exec for all users. If the file affected is a configuration, a binary, a script or sensitive data, it can lead to privilege escalation or information leakage.

IMPROPER_UNICODE.LongDescription: Improper handling of Unicode transformations such as case mapping and normalization.
IMPROPER_UNICODE.Details: Unexpected behavior in unicode transformations can sometimes lead to bugs, some of them affecting software security. A code that applies the uppercase transformation to two strings could mistakenly interpret both strings as being equal.

MODIFICATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.ShortDescription: String is modified after validation and not before it
MODIFICATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.LongDescription: String is modified after validation and not before it. Tricky attackers may pass malicious strings which bypass validation.
MODIFICATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.Details: A string must not be modified after validation because it may allow an attacker to bypass validation using a tricky string which becomes malicious after the modification. For example, a program may filter out the ⟨script⟩ tags from HTML input to avoid cross-site scripting (XSS) and other vulnerabilities. If non-character code points are deleted from the input following validation, an attacker may pass the string "⟨scr"+"\uFDEF"+"ipt⟩" so that the validation check fails to detect the ⟨script⟩ tag, but the subsequent removal of the non-character code pont creates a ⟨script⟩ tag in the input: Pattern pattern = Pattern.compile("<script>"); Matcher matcher = pattern.matcher(s); if (matcher.find()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid input"); } s = s.replaceAll("[\\p{Cn}]", "");

NORMALIZATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.ShortDescription: String is normalized after validation and not before it
NORMALIZATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.LongDescription: String is normalized after validation and not before it. Tricky attackers may pass malicious strings which bypass validation.
NORMALIZATION_AFTER_VALIDATION.Details: A string must not be normalized after validation because it may allow an attacker to bypass validation using a tricky string which becomes malicious after the normalization. For example, a program may filter out the ⟨script⟩ tags from HTML input to avoid cross-site scripting (XSS) and other vulnerabilities. However, in unicode, the same string can have many different representations. For example, \uFE64 is normalized to ⟨ and \uFE65 is normalized to ⟩. Thus, if an attacker passes the string "\uFE64" + "script" + "\uFE65" the validation check fails to detect the ⟨script⟩ tag, but thereafter the string is normalized to the ⟨script⟩ tag in the input: Pattern pattern = Pattern.compile("[<>]"); // Check for angle brackets Matcher matcher = pattern.matcher(s); if (matcher.find()) { throw new IllegalStateException(); } s = Normalizer.normalize(s, Form.NFKC);

DANGEROUS_PERMISSION_COMBINATION.LongDescription: Granting permission {2} on the target {3} in method {1} is dangerous.
DANGEROUS_PERMISSION_COMBINATION.Details: Certain combinations of permissions can produce significant capability increases and should not be granted. Granting ReflectPermission on the target suppressAccessChecks is dangerous in that information (possibly confidential) and methods normally unavailable would be accessible to malicious code. Similarly, the permission java.lang.RuntimePermission applied to target createClassLoader grants code the permission to create a ClassLoader object. This is extremely dangerous, because malicious applications that can instantiate their own class loaders could then load their own rogue classes into the system. These newly loaded classes could be placed into any protection domain by the class loader, thereby automatically granting the classes the permissions for that domain.

POTENTIAL_XML_INJECTION.ShortDescription: An unsafe string is potentially injected into an XML string
POTENTIAL_XML_INJECTION.LongDescription: {3} is potentially injected into an XML string in method {1}.
POTENTIAL_XML_INJECTION.Details: Unsafe strings (e.g. from a user input) may contain XML tags. If such a string is inserted into an XML document then it may change its structure to a valid but semantically different document. To prevent this unsafe strings must b sanitized first.

CT_CONSTRUCTOR_THROW.ShortDescription: Be wary of letting constructors throw exceptions.
CT_CONSTRUCTOR_THROW.LongDescription: Exception thrown in class {0} at {1} will leave the constructor. The object under construction remains partially initialized and may be vulnerable to Finalizer attacks.
CT_CONSTRUCTOR_THROW.Details: Classes that throw exceptions in their constructors are vulnerable to Finalizer attacks. A finalizer attack can be prevented by declaring the class final, using an empty finalizer declared as final, or by a clever use of a private constructor. See SEI CERT Rule OBJ-11 for more information.

JUA_DONT_ASSERT_INSTANCEOF_IN_TESTS.ShortDescription: Asserting value of instanceof in tests is not recommended.
JUA_DONT_ASSERT_INSTANCEOF_IN_TESTS.LongDescription: Assertion of type {0} in {2} at {3} may hide useful information about why a cast may have failed.
JUA_DONT_ASSERT_INSTANCEOF_IN_TESTS.Details: Asserting type checks in tests is not recommended as a class cast exception message could better indicate the cause of an instance of the wrong class being used than an instanceof assertion. When debugging tests that fail due to bad casts, it may be more useful to observe the output of the resulting ClassCastException which could provide information about the actual encountered type. Asserting the type before casting would instead result in a less informative "false is not true" message. If JUnit is used with hamcrest, the IsInstanceOf class from hamcrest could be used instead.

OVERRIDING_METHODS_MUST_INVOKE_SUPER.ShortDescription: Super method is annotated with @OverridingMethodsMustInvokeSuper, but the overriding method isn't calling the super method.
OVERRIDING_METHODS_MUST_INVOKE_SUPER.LongDescription: Super method is annotated with @OverridingMethodsMustInvokeSuper, but {1} isn't calling the super method.
OVERRIDING_METHODS_MUST_INVOKE_SUPER.Details: Super method is annotated with @OverridingMethodsMustInvokeSuper, but the overriding method isn't calling the super method.

CNT_ROUGH_CONSTANT_VALUE.ShortDescription: Rough value of known constant found
CNT_ROUGH_CONSTANT_VALUE.Details: It's recommended to use the predefined library constant for code clarity and better precision. See CWE-1106: Insufficient Use of Symbolic Constants.

SKIPPED_CLASS_TOO_BIG.Details: This class is bigger than can be effectively handled, and was not fully analyzed for errors.

NOISE_NULL_DEREFERENCE.ShortDescription: Bogus warning about a null pointer dereference
NOISE_NULL_DEREFERENCE.LongDescription: Bogus warning about a null pointer dereference in {1}

NOISE_METHOD_CALL.ShortDescription: Bogus warning about a method call
NOISE_METHOD_CALL.LongDescription: Bogus warning about a method call {2} in {1}

NOISE_FIELD_REFERENCE.ShortDescription: Bogus warning about a field reference
NOISE_FIELD_REFERENCE.LongDescription: Bogus warning about a reference to {2} in {1}

DMI_BIGDECIMAL_CONSTRUCTED_FROM_DOUBLE.ShortDescription: BigDecimal constructed from double that isn't represented precisely
DMI_BIGDECIMAL_CONSTRUCTED_FROM_DOUBLE.Details: This code creates a BigDecimal from a double value that doesn't translate well to a decimal number. For example, one might assume that writing new BigDecimal(0.1) in Java creates a BigDecimal which is exactly equal to 0.1 (an unscaled value of 1, with a scale of 1), but it is actually equal to 0.1000000000000000055511151231257827021181583404541015625. You probably want to use the BigDecimal.valueOf(double d) method, which uses the String representation of the double to create the BigDecimal (e.g., BigDecimal.valueOf(0.1) gives 0.1). See CWE-1339: Insufficient Precision or Accuracy of a Real Number.

DMI_DOH.LongDescription: D'oh! A nonsensical invocation of {2.nameAndSignature} in {1}
DMI_DOH.Details: This particular method invocation doesn't make sense, for reasons that should be apparent from inspection.

DMI_VACUOUS_CALL_TO_EASYMOCK_METHOD.Details: This call doesn't pass any objects to the EasyMock method, so the call doesn't do anything.

DMI_SCHEDULED_THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR_WITH_.ShortDescription: Creation of ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor with zero core threads
DMI_SCHEDULED_THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR_WITH_.LongDescription: Creation of ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor with zero core threads in {1}
DMI_SCHEDULED_THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR_WITH_.Details: (Javadoc) A ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor with zero core threads will never execute anything; changes to the max pool size are ignored.

DMI_FUTILE_ATTEMPT_TO_CHANGE_MAXPOOL_SIZ.ShortDescription: Futile attempt to change max pool size of ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor
DMI_FUTILE_ATTEMPT_TO_CHANGE_MAXPOOL_SIZ.LongDescription: Futile attempt to change max pool size of ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor in {1}
DMI_FUTILE_ATTEMPT_TO_CHANGE_MAXPOOL_SIZ.Details: (Javadoc) While ScheduledThreadPoolExecutor inherits from ThreadPoolExecutor, a few of the inherited tuning methods are not useful for it. In particular, because it acts as a fixed-sized pool using corePoolSize threads and an unbounded queue, adjustments to maximumPoolSize have no useful effect.

DMI_UNSUPPORTED_METHOD.Details: All targets of this method invocation throw an UnsupportedOperationException.

DMI_EMPTY_DB_PASSWORD.Details: This code creates a database connect using a blank or empty password. This indicates that the database is not protected by a password. See CWE-259: Use of Hard-coded Password.

DMI_CONSTANT_DB_PASSWORD.Details: This code creates a database connect using a hardcoded, constant password. Anyone with access to either the source code or the compiled code can easily learn the password. See CWE-259: Use of Hard-coded Password.

HRS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_COOKIE.ShortDescription: HTTP cookie formed from untrusted input
HRS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_COOKIE.LongDescription: HTTP cookie formed from untrusted input in {1}
HRS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_COOKIE.Details: This code constructs an HTTP Cookie using an untrusted HTTP parameter. If this cookie is added to an HTTP response, it will allow an HTTP response splitting vulnerability. See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_response_splitting for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of HTTP response splitting. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about HTTP response splitting, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-113: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Request/Response Splitting').

HRS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_HTTP_HEADER.LongDescription: HTTP parameter directly written to HTTP header output in {1}
HRS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_HTTP_HEADER.Details: This code directly writes an HTTP parameter to an HTTP header, which allows for an HTTP response splitting vulnerability. See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_response_splitting for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of HTTP response splitting. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about HTTP response splitting, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-113: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Request/Response Splitting').

PT_RELATIVE_PATH_TRAVERSAL.Details: The software uses an HTTP request parameter to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize sequences such as ".." that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory. See http://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/23.html for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of relative path traversal. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about relative path traversal, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-23: Relative Path Traversal.

PT_ABSOLUTE_PATH_TRAVERSAL.Details: The software uses an HTTP request parameter to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize absolute path sequences such as "/abs/path" that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory. See http://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/36.html for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of absolute path traversal. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about absolute path traversal, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-36: Absolute Path Traversal.

XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SERVLET_WRITER.ShortDescription: Servlet reflected cross site scripting vulnerability
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SERVLET_WRITER.LongDescription: HTTP parameter written to Servlet output in {1}
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SERVLET_WRITER.Details: This code directly writes an HTTP parameter to Servlet output, which allows for a reflected cross site scripting vulnerability. See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_scripting for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of cross site scripting. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more cross site scripting vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about cross site scripting, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting').

XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SEND_ERROR.ShortDescription: Servlet reflected cross site scripting vulnerability in error page
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SEND_ERROR.LongDescription: HTTP parameter written to Servlet error page in {1}
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_SEND_ERROR.Details: This code directly writes an HTTP parameter to a Server error page (using HttpServletResponse.sendError). Echoing this untrusted input allows for a reflected cross site scripting vulnerability. See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_scripting for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of cross site scripting. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more cross site scripting vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about cross site scripting, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-81: Improper Neutralization of Script in an Error Message Web Page and CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting').

XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_JSP_WRITER.ShortDescription: JSP reflected cross site scripting vulnerability
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_JSP_WRITER.LongDescription: HTTP parameter directly written to JSP output, giving reflected XSS vulnerability in {1.class}
XSS_REQUEST_PARAMETER_TO_JSP_WRITER.Details: This code directly writes an HTTP parameter to JSP output, which allows for a cross site scripting vulnerability. See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_scripting for more information. SpotBugs looks only for the most blatant, obvious cases of cross site scripting. If SpotBugs found any, you almost certainly have more cross site scripting vulnerabilities that SpotBugs doesn't report. If you are concerned about cross site scripting, you should seriously consider using a commercial static analysis or pen-testing tool. See CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting').

SW_SWING_METHODS_INVOKED_IN_SWING_THREAD.ShortDescription: Certain swing methods need to be invoked in Swing thread
SW_SWING_METHODS_INVOKED_IN_SWING_THREAD.LongDescription: Call to swing method in {1} needs to be performed in Swing event thread
SW_SWING_METHODS_INVOKED_IN_SWING_THREAD.Details: (From JDC Tech Tip): The Swing methods show(), setVisible(), and pack() will create the associated peer for the frame. With the creation of the peer, the system creates the event dispatch thread. This makes things problematic because the event dispatch thread could be notifying listeners while pack and validate are still processing. This situation could result in two threads going through the Swing component-based GUI -- it's a serious flaw that could result in deadlocks or other related threading issues. A pack call causes components to be realized. As they are being realized (that is, not necessarily visible), they could trigger listener notification on the event dispatch thread.

IL_INFINITE_LOOP.LongDescription: There is an apparent infinite loop in {1}
IL_INFINITE_LOOP.Details: This loop doesn't seem to have a way to terminate (other than by perhaps throwing an exception). Unconditional loops such as while (true) {...} are assumed to be intentional and are not reported as bugs. See CWE-835: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop').

IL_INFINITE_RECURSIVE_LOOP.LongDescription: There is an apparent infinite recursive loop in {1}
IL_INFINITE_RECURSIVE_LOOP.Details: This method unconditionally invokes itself. This would seem to indicate an infinite recursive loop that will result in a stack overflow. See CWE-674: Uncontrolled Recursion and CWE-835: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop').

IL_CONTAINER_ADDED_TO_ITSELF.ShortDescription: A collection is added to itself
IL_CONTAINER_ADDED_TO_ITSELF.LongDescription: A collection is added to itself in {1}
IL_CONTAINER_ADDED_TO_ITSELF.Details: A collection is added to itself. As a result, computing the hashCode of this set will throw a StackOverflowException.

VO_VOLATILE_REFERENCE_TO_ARRAY.ShortDescription: A volatile reference to an array doesn't treat the array elements as volatile
VO_VOLATILE_REFERENCE_TO_ARRAY.LongDescription: {1} is a volatile reference to an array; the array elements are non-volatile
VO_VOLATILE_REFERENCE_TO_ARRAY.Details: This declares a volatile reference to an array, which might not be what you want. With a volatile reference to an array, reads and writes of the reference to the array are treated as volatile, but the array elements are non-volatile. To get volatile array elements, you will need to use one of the atomic array classes in java.util.concurrent (provided in Java 5.0).

VO_VOLATILE_INCREMENT.ShortDescription: An increment to a volatile field isn't atomic
VO_VOLATILE_INCREMENT.Details: This code increments/decrements a volatile field. Increments/Decrements of volatile fields aren't atomic. If more than one thread is incrementing/decrementing the field at the same time, increments/decrements could be lost. See CWE-567: Unsynchronized Access to Shared Data in a Multithreaded Context.

UI_INHERITANCE_UNSAFE_GETRESOURCE.ShortDescription: Usage of GetResource may be unsafe if class is extended
UI_INHERITANCE_UNSAFE_GETRESOURCE.LongDescription: Usage of GetResource in {1} may be unsafe if class is extended
UI_INHERITANCE_UNSAFE_GETRESOURCE.Details: Calling this.getClass().getResource(...) could give results other than expected if this class is extended by a class in another package.

NP_BOOLEAN_RETURN_NULL.ShortDescription: Method with Boolean return type returns explicit null
NP_BOOLEAN_RETURN_NULL.LongDescription: {1} has Boolean return type and returns explicit null
NP_BOOLEAN_RETURN_NULL.Details: A method that returns either Boolean.TRUE, Boolean.FALSE or null is an accident waiting to happen. This method can be invoked as though it returned a value of type boolean, and the compiler will insert automatic unboxing of the Boolean value. If a null value is returned, this will result in a NullPointerException.

NP_OPTIONAL_RETURN_NULL.ShortDescription: Method with Optional return type returns explicit null
NP_OPTIONAL_RETURN_NULL.LongDescription: {1} has Optional return type and returns explicit null
NP_OPTIONAL_RETURN_NULL.Details: The usage of Optional return type (java.util.Optional or com.google.common.base.Optional) always means that explicit null returns were not desired by design. Returning a null value in such case is a contract violation and will most likely break client code.

NP_NONNULL_FIELD_NOT_INITIALIZED_IN_CONS.LongDescription: Non-null field {2.name} is not initialized by {1}
NP_NONNULL_FIELD_NOT_INITIALIZED_IN_CONS.Details: The field is marked as non-null, but isn't written to by the constructor. The field might be initialized elsewhere during constructor, or might always be initialized before use.

NP_SYNC_AND_NULL_CHECK_FIELD.ShortDescription: Synchronize and null check on the same field.
NP_SYNC_AND_NULL_CHECK_FIELD.LongDescription: In {1} the field {2.givenClass} is synchronized on and then checked if null.
NP_SYNC_AND_NULL_CHECK_FIELD.Details: Since the field is synchronized on, it seems not likely to be null. If it is null and then synchronized on a NullPointerException will be thrown and the check would be pointless. Better to synchronize on another field.

RpC_REPEATED_CONDITIONAL_TEST.Details: The code contains a conditional test is performed twice, one right after the other (e.g., x == 0 || x == 0). Perhaps the second occurrence is intended to be something else (e.g., x == 0 || y == 0).

TESTING.Details: This bug pattern is only generated by new, incompletely implemented bug detectors.

TESTING1.Details: This bug pattern is only generated by new, incompletely implemented bug detectors.

TESTING2.Details: This bug pattern is only generated by new, incompletely implemented bug detectors.

TESTING3.Details: This bug pattern is only generated by new, incompletely implemented bug detectors.

UNKNOWN.Details: A warning was recorded, but SpotBugs cannot find the description of this bug pattern and so cannot describe it. This should occur only in cases of a bug in SpotBugs or its configuration, or perhaps if an analysis was generated using a plugin, but that plugin is not currently loaded. .

AM_CREATES_EMPTY_ZIP_FILE_ENTRY.ShortDescription: Creates an empty zip file entry
AM_CREATES_EMPTY_ZIP_FILE_ENTRY.LongDescription: Empty zip file entry created in {1}
AM_CREATES_EMPTY_ZIP_FILE_ENTRY.Details: The code calls putNextEntry(), immediately followed by a call to closeEntry(). This results in an empty ZipFile entry. The contents of the entry should be written to the ZipFile between the calls to putNextEntry() and closeEntry().

AM_CREATES_EMPTY_JAR_FILE_ENTRY.ShortDescription: Creates an empty jar file entry
AM_CREATES_EMPTY_JAR_FILE_ENTRY.LongDescription: Empty jar file entry created in {1}
AM_CREATES_EMPTY_JAR_FILE_ENTRY.Details: The code calls putNextEntry(), immediately followed by a call to closeEntry(). This results in an empty JarFile entry. The contents of the entry should be written to the JarFile between the calls to putNextEntry() and closeEntry().

IMSE_DONT_CATCH_IMSE.Details: IllegalMonitorStateException is generally only thrown in case of a design flaw in your code (calling wait or notify on an object you do not hold a lock on).

FL_MATH_USING_FLOAT_PRECISION.ShortDescription: Method performs math using floating point precision
FL_MATH_USING_FLOAT_PRECISION.LongDescription: {1} performs math using floating point precision
FL_MATH_USING_FLOAT_PRECISION.Details: The method performs math operations using floating point precision. Floating point precision is very imprecise. For example, 16777216.0f + 1.0f = 16777216.0f. Consider using double math instead. See CWE-1339: Insufficient Precision or Accuracy of a Real Number.

CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_FIELD.ShortDescription: Covariant array assignment to a field
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_FIELD.LongDescription: Array of type {2} is assigned to the field of type {3}
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_FIELD.Details: Array of covariant type is assigned to a field. This is confusing and may lead to ArrayStoreException at runtime if the reference of some other type will be stored in this array later like in the following code: Number[] arr = new Integer[10]; arr[0] = 1.0; Consider changing the type of created array or the field type.

CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_LOCAL.ShortDescription: Covariant array assignment to a local variable
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_LOCAL.LongDescription: Array of type {2} is assigned to the variable of type {3}
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_LOCAL.Details: Array of covariant type is assigned to a local variable. This is confusing and may lead to ArrayStoreException at runtime if the reference of some other type will be stored in this array later like in the following code: Number[] arr = new Integer[10]; arr[0] = 1.0; Consider changing the type of created array or the local variable type.

CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_RETURN.ShortDescription: Covariant array is returned from the method
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_RETURN.LongDescription: Array of type {2} is returned from the method which return type is {3}
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_RETURN.Details: Array of covariant type is returned from the method. This is confusing and may lead to ArrayStoreException at runtime if the calling code will try to store the reference of some other type in the returned array. Consider changing the type of created array or the method return type.

CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_ELEMENT_STORE.ShortDescription: Possibly incompatible element is stored in covariant array
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_ELEMENT_STORE.LongDescription: Value of type {2} is stored into array which element type is {3}
CAA_COVARIANT_ARRAY_ELEMENT_STORE.Details: Value is stored into the array and the value type doesn't match the array type. It's known from the analysis that actual array type is narrower than the declared type of its variable or field and this assignment doesn't satisfy the original array type. This assignment may cause ArrayStoreException at runtime.

CN_IDIOM.ShortDescription: Class implements Cloneable but does not define or use clone method
CN_IDIOM.LongDescription: Class {0} implements Cloneable but does not define or use clone method
CN_IDIOM.Details: Class implements Cloneable but does not define or use the clone method.

CN_IMPLEMENTS_CLONE_BUT_NOT_CLONEABLE.ShortDescription: Class defines clone() but doesn't implement Cloneable
CN_IMPLEMENTS_CLONE_BUT_NOT_CLONEABLE.LongDescription: {0} defines clone() but doesn't implement Cloneable
